Troubleshooting

Why Are My Windows Streaky After Cleaning? Root Causes and Real Fixes

Published 2026-06-01 · Wagon Windows

Finishing a window clean and seeing streaks is one of the most frustrating outcomes in household maintenance. The problem feels simple but the cause isn't always obvious — and applying the wrong fix to the wrong cause makes it worse. Here's how to diagnose which streak problem you actually have, and how to resolve each one cleanly.

Need professional window cleaning in the Shuswap?

Book Online — Free Quote (403) 837-5092

The Five Root Causes of Streaky Windows

Before reaching for the spray bottle again, it's worth understanding that streaks come from fundamentally different sources, each with a different solution. Treating them all the same way — spray more, wipe harder — is why the streak problem persists for so many people. **Cause 1: Wrong solution concentration.** Window cleaning solutions need to be diluted correctly to work properly. Too concentrated and the solution leaves a soapy film when it dries; too dilute and it doesn't cut the oils and biofilm on the glass surface, leaving residue behind. Most DIY cleaners either use undiluted dish soap (too concentrated, too much surfactant) or overly diluted mixtures. Professionals use purpose-formulated window cleaning solution at the correct dilution ratio for the temperature and glass condition. **Cause 2: Dirty squeegee rubber.** A squeegee rubber blade accumulates grit, dried cleaning solution, and glass-surface oils with every pass. On a dirty rubber, the contamination is redistributed across the glass rather than cleared. This is the single most common cause of streaks in DIY cleaning. The rubber should be wiped with a clean damp cloth between passes, and replaced when it shows nicks, hardening, or permanent deformation along the edge. **Cause 3: Detailing cloth that's not dry enough.** The 'detail' step — edging and corner work with a microfiber cloth — is where most streaks are introduced. If the cloth is damp from a previous use, or was left in a wet state, it redistributes moisture rather than absorbing it. Detailing cloths should be dry and clean. In professional cleaning, multiple cloths are staged so there's always a fresh dry one available. **Cause 4: Technique timing.** In warm weather or direct sun, solution dries faster than the squeegee can remove it. When dried solution is pushed by the squeegee, it streaks. The fix is to work in the shade when possible, work faster, or add a small amount of solution to slow the drying time. In cold weather, the reverse problem occurs — solution dries too slowly and needs extra detailing work. **Cause 5: Mineral-laden water.** Hard water contains dissolved calcium and magnesium. When water dries on glass, these minerals are left behind as white, chalky deposits. If you're mixing cleaning solution with tap water in a hard-water area — like much of the Shuswap region — the minerals in your mixing water can streak even with perfect technique.

  • Wrong solution concentration: too soapy = film; too dilute = oily residue
  • Dirty squeegee rubber: redistributes contamination instead of removing it
  • Damp detailing cloth: smears moisture instead of absorbing it
  • Timing: direct sun or heat dries solution before squeegee completes the pass
  • Hard water: dissolved minerals left on glass when tap water is used undiltered

How to Diagnose Which Problem You Have

Streaks look similar to the naked eye, but the pattern tells you the source if you know what to look for. **Soap film streaks** look like a uniform milky haze across the glass, not individual lines. They don't have a directional pattern. They're worse in areas where more solution was applied. When you breathe on the glass, the fogging is uneven — soapy areas fog differently than clean areas. Fix: reduce solution concentration significantly. Start with 1-2 drops of dish soap in a 4-litre bucket. **Dirty rubber streaks** appear as parallel lines following the path of the squeegee stroke. The pattern is directional and consistent with the squeegee direction. The lines repeat at roughly equal intervals, matching the length of the rubber blade. Fix: wipe the rubber edge thoroughly, test on a corner pane, and if the blade is damaged or hardened, replace it. **Detailing cloth streaks** appear in corners, along the edge where rubber meets frame, and at the bottom of the pane where detail work is done. They're often curved or irregular — the shape follows the cloth wiping motion rather than the squeegee direction. Fix: switch to a dry microfiber cloth and work from the corner outward with light pressure. **Hard water streaks** are most visible in raking sunlight at an angle. They appear as white or grayish haze that doesn't wipe off with a dry cloth. They're most concentrated near the bottom of the pane, where rinse water flows and dries. Fix: use distilled water for mixing, or apply a diluted white vinegar solution (1:1 with water) to dissolve mineral deposits before the main clean.

If you've corrected technique, solution, and tools and still have streaks, read section four — the problem may not be with the cleaning at all.

Fixing Each Streak Type: The Practical Steps

Once you've diagnosed the cause, the fix is straightforward. **For soap film**: Completely rinse the glass with clean water to remove existing residue. Mix fresh solution at a much lower concentration — one small drop of dish soap per litre of water, or use a commercial window cleaning concentrate at the manufacturer's dilution. Re-clean from scratch. **For dirty rubber streaks**: Buy a replacement squeegee rubber. They're inexpensive and this is the single best investment in DIY window cleaning quality. Install it correctly — blade seated evenly, no wobble — and wipe it between passes with a clean damp cloth. Hold the squeegee at a consistent angle (about 30 degrees) throughout each stroke. **For detailing cloth streaks**: Use only dry, lint-free microfiber cloths. Wring them out fully if they've been used previously, then hang to dry before reuse. For corner detail work, fold the cloth to a fresh quadrant for each corner. Work from the top corner down with light pressure. **For hard water mineral streaks**: Mix a 1:1 white vinegar and water solution. Apply to the affected glass with a cloth, allow one minute of dwell time, then wipe off. The acid in the vinegar dissolves calcium carbonate deposits that water alone can't remove. Follow with a standard window clean to remove the vinegar residue. For severe mineral buildup, a commercial calcium remover product may be needed before this step.

  • Soap film: rinse completely, remake solution at much lower concentration
  • Dirty rubber: replace the rubber blade, wipe between passes
  • Cloth streaks: always use dry lint-free microfiber, fresh quadrant per corner
  • Hard water: white vinegar 1:1 pre-treatment dissolves calcium deposits

When the Glass Is the Problem, Not the Cleaning

If you've corrected all five variables and still have streaks or haze, the glass itself may be the issue. This is more common than most homeowners realize, and it's important to identify it correctly so you don't waste time trying to clean away something that isn't a cleaning problem. **Old or weathered glass**: Glass surfaces develop micro-abrasions over many years of weathering, cleaning, and UV exposure. These micro-abrasions scatter light at angles that create a hazy appearance. This is most visible on south-facing and west-facing windows with high UV exposure. There's no cleaning solution for micro-abrasion; it's a glass surface condition that can only be addressed by glass polishing compounds (specialized products) or ultimately window replacement. **Etched glass surface**: Repeated exposure to hard water deposits that aren't fully removed causes glass surface etching over time. Unlike mineral deposits that sit on the surface, etching is actual surface damage — the calcium has chemically reacted with the silica in the glass. Etched glass has a permanently hazy appearance that doesn't improve with cleaning. Mild etching can sometimes be improved with glass polishing compounds; severe etching typically requires replacement. **Failed window seal (fogging between panes)**: If the haze appears to be inside the glass — specifically between the two panes of a double-pane window — this is a failed insulated glass unit seal, not a cleaning issue. No amount of cleaning addresses this. See our guide on foggy windows for the full explanation and remediation path.

  • Old/weathered glass: micro-abrasions create permanent haze not removable by cleaning
  • Etched glass: calcium has chemically reacted with glass surface — cleaning won't help
  • Failed window seal: haze between panes is a structural repair issue, not a cleaning issue

Frequently Asked Questions

A professional cleaner left streaks on my windows. What should I do?

Contact the company immediately and describe the issue specifically — where the streaks are, what they look like, when you noticed them. Professional window cleaners should stand behind their work with a satisfaction guarantee. A reputable company will return to address streaks at no charge. If the company is unresponsive or dismissive, that's a service quality red flag worth noting in a review.

Why do my windows look worse after cleaning than before?

The most common cause is that cleaning with incorrect solution or technique has redistributed existing contamination rather than removing it. Another possibility is that the glass has existing mineral deposits or haze that were hidden by surface grime — once the surface layer is removed, the underlying condition becomes visible. Starting with a white vinegar pre-treatment, rinsing thoroughly, and re-cleaning with fresh solution usually resolves the first problem.

Does the brand of window cleaner matter for avoiding streaks?

More than the brand, the concentration matters. Commercial products like Windex or Method work well when applied correctly — but 'correctly' usually means less product than most people use. Excess cleaner is the primary cause of streaky DIY cleans. Professional window cleaners use very dilute purpose-formulated solutions, not consumer spray products applied heavily.

Is there a way to make windows stay streak-free longer?

Yes — hydrophobic glass treatments (Rain-X and similar products) create a water-shedding surface that causes water to bead and run off rather than spreading and drying. Applied after a thorough clean, these products reduce water spotting and slow re-dirtying. They need re-application every few months but significantly extend the clean appearance between professional visits.
